behaviors.description: Speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so attendees can be attributed in the summary
behaviors.justification: The real failure—speaker labels being dropped, reformatted, or inconsistently returned by the actual speech-to-text API—cannot be caught by mocks. The API's actual behavior with real audio and real label handling must be tested live. A mock can always return labels in the expected format, hiding edge cases where the real API fails on certain audio conditions, speaker overlap, or format variations.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real speech-to-text API with a multi-speaker audio file containing clear speaker transitions. Verify the API returns speaker labels, extract those labels, and confirm they are preserved through the pipeline so the LLM receives attribution data for each speaker.

behaviors.description: Structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee assigned a task
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends entirely on real LLM behavior—the LLM must correctly identify all attendees, recognize task assignment language, and attribute each task to the right person. This is non-deterministic: the same transcript run twice through a real LLM might produce slightly different action item extractions. Mocking the LLM to always return correct output completely hides real extraction failures, missed attendees, and misattributions.
behaviors.test_description: Create a meeting transcript with 3+ attendees and explicit task assignments (e.g., 'Alice, can you follow up with legal?' 'Bob will handle the API refactor'). Send to real LLM for summarization. Verify the summary contains at least one action item for each attendee explicitly assigned work.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in narrative
behaviors.justification: Real LLMs vary in how they categorize and extract content. The LLM might fail to recognize decision language, might categorize a decision as narrative rather than a key decision, or might miss decisions entirely. This failure is specific to LLM behavior and non-deterministic—running the test 100 times might yield different categorization results. A mock LLM always places decisions in the right section, hiding real categorization failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a meeting transcript with 3+ explicit decisions (e.g., 'We decided to migrate to PostgreSQL' 'The team agreed to ship Q2'). Send to real LLM for summarization. Verify each decision appears in the summary's 'decisions' section, not left in the narrative or body text.

behaviors.description: Audio longer than 60 minutes is split into segments, processed sequentially, and merged into a single coherent summary without losing content or speaker attribution
behaviors.justification: While merge logic is local (Integration-testable), mocking the speech-to-text API hides real failures: the real API might return speaker labels in different formats across segments, drop labels entirely on the second segment, or introduce inconsistencies that break the merge. Real long audio also triggers edge cases (segmentation at awkward boundaries, speaker ID resets). Only the real API reveals these format inconsistencies and real audio edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Upload a real audio file >60 minutes with multiple speakers and clear speaker changes. Verify the service splits it into segments, sends each to the real speech-to-text API, and merges results. Assert no content is lost, speaker labels are consistent across segments, and the final transcript flows logically without gaps or duplicates.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline from audio upload through transcription, summarization, and structured output delivery completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording
behaviors.justification: This is a complete agentic workflow: real audio upload (System) → real speech-to-text transcription (System) → real LLM summarization (Agentic) → output delivery (System). Failures can occur at any stage or in the orchestration between them. Testing at System level alone misses LLM-specific failures (non-deterministic output, rate limits, hallucination). Testing at Agentic level alone misses transcription errors and orchestration gaps. The full pipeline must work end-to-end with all real dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Upload a real meeting recording through the full pipeline. Verify transcription completes with speaker labels, LLM summarization generates structured output (decisions, action items, attendees), and the final result is delivered in the expected format without errors or manual intervention.
